Summary of follow-up action taken to implement the decisions and requests of the General Assembly in its resolutions 76/274 and 79/312, including the requests and recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions endorsed by the Assembly

Summary of follow-up action taken to implement the decisions and requests of the General Assembly in its resolutions 76/274 and 79/312, including the requests and recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions endorsed by the Assembly

Summary of follow-up action taken to implement the decisions and requests of the General Assembly in its resolutions 76/274 and 79/312, including the requests and recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions endorsed by the Assembly

The present report contains the budget performance and the budget for the United Nations Support Office in Somalia (UNSOS) for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 respectively.

During the 2024/25 period, UNSOS played a key role in the transition from the African Union Transition Mission in Somalia (ATMIS) to the African Union Support and Stabilization Mission in Somalia (AUSSOM) and successfully completed phase 3 of the ATMIS drawdown through the handover of three forward operating bases, the repatriation of contingents and environmentally compliant asset disposal. The mission provided non-lethal logistical support to AUSSOM and Somali security forces to advance peace and security in Somalia. UNSOS also delivered capacity-building training and ensured compliance with the human rights due diligence policy. Notwithstanding persistent security challenges, UNSOS fulfilled its mandate, contributing to stability and institutional strengthening in Somalia.

During the 2026/27 budget period, and in accordance with Security Council resolution 2809 (2025), UNSOS will continue to align its strategic focus with evolving operational requirements The mission’s priorities will focus on providing logistical support to AUSSOM, support for Somali security forces and the delivery of administrative and logistical support to UNTMIS until its planned closure by 31 October 2026, and thereafter its in situ liquidation.

During the 2024/25 period, UNSOS incurred $486.3 million in expenditure for the budget performance period, representing a resource utilization rate of 97.3 per cent, compared with $533.0 million in expenditure and a resource utilization rate of 96.6 per cent in the 2023/24 period.

The unencumbered balance of $13.5 million reflects the net impact of: (a) increased requirements for military and police personnel, attributable primarily to the continued deployment of 11,622 ATMIS/AUSSOM personnel throughout the 2024/25 budget period, as authorized by the Security Council in its resolutions 2748 (2024), compared with the budgeted deployment of 9,586 ATMIS/AUSSOM personnel; (b) increased requirements for civilian personnel, attributable primarily to the lower vacancy rates and higher salary rates compared with budgeted rates; and (c) reduced requirements for operational costs, attributable primarily to: (i) the lower costs for the rental and operation of aircraft due to the non-utilization of shared C-130 aircraft and the non-deployment of four military helicopters; and (ii) the prioritization of resources aimed at curtailing operational costs in response to liquidity challenges.

The budget proposed for the 2026/27 period in the amount of $481,671,800 represents a decrease of $38,956,100, or 7.5 per cent, compared with the apportionment of $520,627,900 approved for the 2025/26 period.

The estimates for the 2026/27 period reflect the net impact of: (a) increased requirements under military and police personnel, attributable mainly to higher freight and travel costs associated with the anticipated repatriation of military-owned equipment and its subsequent deployment of new troop-contributing countries, in accordance with the planned AUSSOM reconfiguration; (b) increased requirements under civilian personnel, attributable to the application of a lower vacancy rate than that applied in the approved budget, as well as the establishment of nine posts related to the human rights due diligence policy, a function that UNSOS is now required to assume with the closure of UNTMIS; and (c) reduced requirements under operational costs, reflecting efficiencies and reductions in scope across budget classes, air operations, facilities and infrastructure, information and communications technology, mine action activities and logistics, including the reconfiguration of aviation assets.
(Thousands of United States dollars; budget year is from 1 July to 30 June)

Human resourcesa

text: I.	Mandate, results achieved and planned outcome

children.text: A.	Mandate

1.	The United Nations Support Office in Somalia (UNSOS) was established by the Security Council in its resolution 1863 (2009) to provide support to the African Union Mission in Somalia. The mandate for the performance period was provided in its resolutions 2748 (2024), 2767 (2024) and 2776 (2025). The most recent extension of the mandate was authorized by the Council in its resolution 2809 (2025), by which it extended the mandate until 31 December 2026.

2.	The mission is mandated to help the Security Council to advance a multi-year strategic vision to strengthen peace, security and stability in Somalia. This includes providing logistical support to the African Union Support and Stabilization Mission in Somalia (AUSSOM), conducting joint operations and enhancing the operational capacity of Somali security forces. The objective is to provide critical logistical, aviation and operational support to enable the implementation of mandate priorities under the Somalia security development plan, in full compliance with the human rights due diligence policy. The Council, in its resolution 2809 (2025), also reaffirmed the continued role of UNSOS in providing required logistical and administrative services, including to the United Nations Transitional Assistance Mission in Somalia (UNTMIS) on a cost-recovery basis until the conclusion of its phased drawdown by 31 October 2026.
B.	Budget implementation for 2024/25

3.	During the reporting period, the security environment in Somalia remained highly volatile, characterized by persistent asymmetric threats, including improvised explosive devices, indirect fire and emerging uncrewed aerial vehicle risks, in particular in Banaadir, Hirshabelle, Lower Shabelle, Bay and Jubaland. Notwithstanding those conditions, UNSOS maintained uninterrupted mandate delivery through adaptive operational planning, enhanced force protection and close coordination with Somali authorities, the African Union and United Nations partners.

4.	UNSOS played a central role in enabling the phase 3 drawdown of the African Union Transition Mission in Somalia (ATMIS), the transition to AUSSOM and the transition from the United Nations Assistance Mission in Somalia (UNSOM) to UNTMIS, ensuring continuity of security coverage and logistical support in a high-risk environment. Through coordinated handover processes, UNSOS supported the transfer of three forward operating bases to Somali security forces, reinforcing national ownership under the Somalia Transition Plan and contributing directly to the progressive assumption of security responsibilities by national institutions.

5.	In response to evolving threats, including increased uncrewed aerial vehicle activity with suspected hostile intent, UNSOS, in collaboration with the Department of Safety and Security and international partners, strengthened early warning and counter-uncrewed aerial vehicle capabilities, improving situational awareness and response. Infrastructure upgrades at key UNSOS locations included reinforced overhead protection, bunkers and perimeter fortifications, significantly reducing vulnerability to indirect fire and enhancing the safety of AUSSOM and United Nations personnel.

6.	UNSOS ensured operational continuity through critical logistical and aviation support. The mission repatriated uniformed personnel and associated contingent-owned equipment and transferred United Nations-owned items to Somali security forces, in the context of the closure or handover of forward operating bases to the Federal Government of Somalia. Notwithstanding financial constraints, UNSOS maintained essential support through asset repurposing, the use of renewable energy and local procurement, demonstrating operational agility and prudent resource management. Support for the initial deployment of AUSSOM personnel ensured uninterrupted troop rotations, casualty evacuation and supply chain operations, enabling a seamless transition from ATMIS to AUSSOM and reinforcing confidence among partners and stakeholders.

7.	UNSOS maintained reliable mission infrastructure and essential services across all locations. Power, water and facility services were sustained, while prefabricated offices, towers, bunkers and perimeter structures were constructed, as required. Geohydrogeological surveys enabled the drilling of three boreholes, in Balcad, Xawaadley and Kismaayo. Wastewater treatment, water treatment and distribution, sanitation systems, and mission-wide water quality monitoring were maintained, ensuring safe and sustainable operational environments.

8.	Aviation assets remained a key force multiplier, enabling troop mobility, supply delivery and medical evacuations. The deployment of tactical uncrewed aircraft systems enhanced intelligence, situational awareness and threat response, strengthening operational effectiveness and troop confidence. Combined with airlift and attack helicopters, those assets improved the mission’s ability to respond to Al-Shabaab threats, conduct joint operations and carry out medical evacuations. Aviation support therefore remained critical in sustaining operational tempo and enabling AUSSOM to meet mandate objectives under the Somalia Transition Plan.

9.	UNSOS strengthened environmental sustainability and risk mitigation across mission operations. Compliance with United Nations environmental standards was ensured through site assessments, audits and training. Renewable energy capacity was increased by installing 150 kW of solar panels in Kismaayo, while energy-saving measures such as generator synchronization, LED lighting and motion sensors reduced fuel consumption. Waste management at AUSSOM forward operating bases improved through the deployment of portable dry toilets and incinerators. Reforestation efforts advanced through tree seeding and the introduction of drone-assisted seed ball drops, enhancing national capacity for environmental restoration.

10.	UNSOS strengthened public confidence in the security transition through a comprehensive communications strategy aligned with the Somalia Transition Plan. The successful rebranding from ATMIS to AUSSOM, supported by coordinated multimedia campaigns and community engagement, significantly enhanced mission visibility and public awareness. To reinforce Somali ownership, UNSOS prioritized national participation in communications delivery, maintaining six community radio transmitter sites, in Baidoa, Beledweyne, Kismaayo, Jawhar, Dhooble and Dhuusamarreeb. Those platforms expanded outreach to local communities, while partner engagement and the distribution of publicity materials further enhanced awareness of the transition process.

11.	Mine action support was critical to enabling safe drawdown and transition activities. Extensive training, mentoring and deployment of explosive detection assets significantly improved force protection and operational readiness for ATMIS, AUSSOM and Somali security forces. Those efforts saved lives directly, safeguarded assets and enabled freedom of movement in high-threat environments. A total of 1,252 troops were trained in explosive ordnance disposal, 788 in explosive hazard awareness, 1,244 in combat medic skills, 637 in specialized technical and support skills and 658 in search capability skills, substantially strengthening the mission’s operational readiness and the self-sufficiency of troop-contributing countries. That training directly enhanced the ability of personnel to respond to threats, save lives in combat situations and support mission-critical operations. In addition, the deployment of 42 explosive detection dog teams played a key role in mitigating security risks, given that the teams responded to 100 per cent of all tasking requests conducting thorough searches of luggage, vehicles, buildings, building areas and open terrain. This significantly contributed to force protection, safeguarding United Nations personnel, assets and local populations.

12.	Capacity-building efforts further enhanced the self-reliance and operational readiness of Somali security forces. UNSOS provided specialized training, mentoring and equipment across key areas, including logistics, aviation, medical services, information and communications technology (ICT), engineering, human rights compliance and improvised explosive device disposal. Support included the establishment and training of new improvised explosive device disposal teams, refresher training for existing teams and initiatives to advance sustainable logistics through local ration procurement with escorts provided by Somali security forces. Those measures strengthened the ability of the Somali security forces to detect, respond to and mitigate explosive threats, enabling national forces to assume greater security responsibilities from ATMIS.
C.	Planning assumptions and mission support initiatives for 2026/27
Security situation in the mission area

13.	UNSOS will continue to operate in a complex environment characterized by a fragile security situation, with a high frequency of security incidents anticipated, based on prevailing trends. The majority of past security incidents in Somalia have been attributed to Al-Shabaab, which has demonstrated the capability and intent to conduct high-impact attacks against government institutions, security forces and civilian populations, in particular in urban centres such as Mogadishu. The United Nations continues to face both direct threats and the possibility of being affected by the broader security environment.

14.	With the end of the UNTMIS mandate on 31 October 2026 and the anticipated withdrawal of the United Nations Guard Unit, which provides static armed security for United Nations personnel, premises and assets, a security capability gap is expected. At present, the mission is reviewing options to ensure continuity of essential armed security services after the UNTMIS closure. These options may include the continuation or redeployment of a Guard Unit under a different arrangement or the engagement of an armed private security company, in line with the United Nations Security Management System Security Policy Manual and related guidelines. The proposed budget for 2026/27 includes a provision under security services to accommodate either scenario to ensure a continuity of security services without operational disruption.
Provision of the logistical support package to the African Union Support and Stabilization Mission in Somalia

15.	For the 2026/27 period, UNSOS support will continue to be aligned with the phased implementation of the AUSSOM strategic concept of operations (2024), taking into account lessons learned from the completion of phase 1 and the commencement of Phase 2 Support will be tailored to the evolving operational requirements of AUSSOM and the broader security context. AUSSOM and the Federal Government of Somalia and the troop-contributing countries are expected to operate under a harmonized and integrated force posture. This enhanced cohesion is expected to support an optimized disposition across the 49 operational locations maintained by AUSSOM, all of which will continue to require logistical support from UNSOS. In this context, and consistent with paragraph 23 of resolution 2809 (2025), UNSOS will continue to adapt its support for AUSSOM, taking into account measures implemented to manage its liquidity shortfall and prioritizing the sustained capacity of AUSSOM to consolidate peace and security gains across phases 2, 3 and 4 of the concept of operations. UNSOS will initiate and complete the construction of the second main operating base at Waajid, located in Sector 3, in line with AUSSOM reconfiguration requirements.

16.	During the implementation of phase 2 of the strategic concept of operations, UNSOS will continue to effectively sustain the AUSSOM presence at strategically critical locations, including key infrastructure and population centres where security remains essential to stability. UNSOS will adapt its support modalities to meet operational demands, including the provision of enabling capabilities in support of the campaign plan of Somali security forces against Al-Shabaab. Building on the established support architecture across the mission area, UNSOS will further streamline its logistical framework to accommodate the expanded coordination of AUSSOM with Somali security forces.

17.	In close collaboration with troop- and police-contributing countries, UNSOS will strengthen planning and execution processes by engaging early to identify requirements for the deployment, rotation and repatriation of contingent-owned equipment, ensuring its timely and efficient movement. UNSOS will also pursue operational efficiencies, including the potential adoption of reverse-rotation strategies to reduce empty return flights and to leverage its transportation assets to deliver efficient and responsive movement control services. UNSOS will continue to support the deployment, rotation and repatriation of AUSSOM military and police personnel and equipment.

18.	This strategy is anchored in the decentralization of logistical support at the joint sector hubs, with strategic reinforcement provided by the Mogadishu logistics base at Aden Adde International Airport and the rear support base in Mombasa, Kenya. UNSOS will continue to provide decentralized warehouse support to AUSSOM across all regions, with an increasing focus on strengthening reverse logistics functions to enhance inventory management, reduce stock levels, optimize warehouse capacity and improve asset visibility.
Support for Somali security forces

19.	UNSOS will continue to provide mandated logistics support to Somali security forces engaged in joint or coordinated operations with AUSSOM through the United Nations trust fund in support of the operations of Somali security forces. Subject to new contributions to the trust fund, sustainable support is planned for a ceiling of 20,900 personnel.

20.	In response to the prevailing funding limitations, UNSOS will apply a phased and prioritized approach to support the strengthening of Somali security forces’ operational readiness, sustainability and self-reliance in logistics. In close coordination with the Federal Government of Somalia, these efforts will focus on enhancing internal planning and logistics management capacities, while aligning the scope and sequencing of support with available resources. This includes continued training and advisory support in supply chain management, aviation operations, field technology and liaison coordination, aligned with the evolving requirements of the AUSSOM strategic concept of operations. Insufficient and unsustainable voluntary contributions remain a key risk to the trust fund supporting the operations of the Somali security forces. To address potential funding shortfalls, UNSOS, in coordination with the Federal Government, will strengthen engagement with existing and prospective donors to mobilize additional contributions and broaden the trust fund’s use.
Preparation for the United Nations Transitional Assistance Mission in Somalia transition

21.	In its resolution 2753 (2024), the Security Council expressed its intention to terminate the mandate of UNTMIS at the end of the anticipated two-phased transition on 31 October 2026. Following the UNTMIS closure, UNSOS will play a key role in supporting the transition, from the withdrawal to the liquidation period. UNSOS will implement the mission support transition plan, including the reduction of its physical footprint, an environmental cleanup, asset disposal and administrative support in line with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations and applicable policies. In addition, requirements for a specific essential function, such as security services and medical services, will need to be fully covered by UNSOS from 1 November 2026, compared with the current cost-sharing arrangement.

22.	Pursuant to resolution 2809 (2025), the implementation of the human rights due diligence policy remains mandatory for all United Nations support provided to AUSSOM and Somali security forces. Following the closure of UNTMIS, which has, to date, been mandated to implement the policy in Somalia, UNSOS will assume full responsibility for its implementation and the continuity of risk assessment and mitigation, policy monitoring and analysis and the coordination of policy mechanisms. The human rights due diligence policy task force in Somalia will operate under the joint oversight of the Assistant Secretary-General of UNSOS and the Resident Coordinator and Humanitarian Coordinator, providing strategic direction, policy guidance and operational oversight, while maintaining engagement with Somali authorities, AUSSOM, Somali security forces and international partners.

23.	In this context, the human rights due diligence policy function in UNSOS will comprise 13 posts as from 1 November 2026. Taking into account the need for independence, security and expertise, such capacity in Somalia will be supported by four international and nine national staff. Of these 13 posts, 2 programme management officers are reflected in the current staffing table of UNSOS, while 2 National Professional Officer posts will be provided through the United Nations country team. The nine remaining posts represent additional capacity to be established within UNSOS, in order to enable the mission to assume full responsibilities relating to the policy that were previously undertaken by UNTMIS.
Mission support initiatives

24.	UNSOS will intensify efforts to enhance cost-effectiveness, reinforce accountability and ensure efficient, mission-aligned service delivery throughout 2026/27. Building on existing efficiencies, and in response to the current liquidity situation, UNSOS has advanced planning for an asset optimization review as a forward-looking efficiency measure. Given the need for engagement with relevant partners on revised arrangements, the timing does not allow for savings to be reported in the current budget report. However, if these projected efficiencies are realized, it will be reported in the UNSOS performance report for 2026/27.

25.	After the closure of UNTMIS, UNSOS will reconfigure and reduce its air assets in line with the downsized scope of work, while continuing to provide support through 3 fixed-wing aircraft, 23 rotary-wing aircraft and 1 uncrewed aerial system. These aviation assets will act as critical force multipliers in operations against Al-Shabaab and other extremist organizations by providing combat and logistical support, including resupply missions, quick-reaction force deployments, casualty and medical evacuations, armed escorts, long-range intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance, crewed and uncrewed aerial system operations, and cargo airlifts.

26.	UNSOS will provide engineering and maintenance support to AUSSOM at all locations, including the upkeep and refurbishment of facilities, the operation and maintenance of United Nations-owned water and sanitation systems and the construction and maintenance of storm-drainage and flood-mitigation measures. UNSOS will finalize the construction of perimeter security features such as inner-ring security barriers, bunkers, boom gates, reinforced concrete walls, fences and Hesco Bastion walls.

27.	In support of environmental sustainability and operational resilience, UNSOS will install additional solar-powered water treatment plants, complemented by solar photovoltaic systems aimed at reducing the mission’s carbon footprint. UNSOS plans to procure portable chlorine generators to enable efficient and easier transportation, on-site water treatment and enhance operational flexibility in remote locations.

28.	UNSOS plans to expand the Unite field remote infrastructure monitoring system to include fuel level and flows for the diesel generators and continue to enhance monitoring and reporting of water production supply and consumption, treated wastewater production and electricity generation at all UNSOS sites. UNSOS will conduct comprehensive environmental management training to AUSSOM, focusing on responsible waste disposal, the efficient use of natural resources, compliance with environmental protection standards and the safe handling of hazardous materials.

29.	In parallel, UNSOS will collaborate closely with AUSSOM to advance strategic communications and broader capacity-building objectives. Support will encompass internal and external communications, press and media engagements, multimedia and broadcast productions, community outreach and partner coordination.

30.	UNSOS mine action will continue to enhance the safety, mobility and operational effectiveness of AUSSOM forces operating in a high improvised explosive device threat environment, providing specialized technical support, training, mentoring and advisory services to facilitate secure troop movements and base operations. The programme will emphasize the building of sustainable national capacity through a train-the-trainer approach, integrating improvised explosive device threat mitigation into the predeployment training systems of troop-contributing countries. Support for Somali security forces will include specialized training, equipment provision, the formation of new improvised explosive device mitigation teams, mentoring and the development of qualified national trainers. In addition, mine action will strengthen institutional capacity by supporting the Somali Explosive Management Authority and providing weapons and ammunition management training in line with the related national strategy.

31.	UNSOS will digitalize the disposal monitoring process, ensuring effective asset management and accountability during the period of transition and closure. The digitalization of disposal monitoring will offer several key benefits, including improved data accuracy, real-time tracking of asset status, streamlined reporting and enhanced transparency.

32.	Capacity-building initiatives for national staff will remain a priority, reflecting the Organization’s commitment to strengthening local expertise and promoting sustainability beyond the mission’s eventual closure. Planned activities include a provision for professional certifications, technical skills training and cross-cutting programmes aimed at enhancing employability and facilitating career transition.

33.	In line with the UN80 Initiative, the budget proposal for UNSOS incorporates a set of targeted efficiency measures to be implemented through cost optimization, organizational optimization, functional streamlining and allocative efficiencies. These measures were identified following a comprehensive review of operational requirements, workload distribution and support functions, drawing on the independent strategic review and contingency measures, with the objective of preserving the mission’s capacity to deliver core mandated activities while operating within a reduced resource envelope.

34.	Complementary efficiency gains will be achieved through utility and resource optimization, including adjustments to water production schedules, expanded use of recycled wastewater for non-potable purposes, the rationalization of telephony services, the optimization of ICT infrastructure and the transition of selected outsourced services to in-house delivery, reducing operating, maintenance and environmental footprints.

35.	The proposed civilian staffing complement reflects a reduction of 46 posts and positions, including the abolishment of positions in relation to the closure of UNTMIS and the streamlining of service delivery to align capacity with evolving operational requirements. In parallel, UNSOS is advancing its nationalization strategy by converting 12 posts and positions to Somali nationals, strengthening long-term capacity and enabling the progressive transfer of responsibilities.

36.	The proposed efficiency measures will strengthen the ability of UNSOS to deliver its mandate under financial constraints by reallocating resources to priority requirements. These measures, which are implemented in close collaboration with AUSSOM, will facilitate the ongoing reconfiguration and the deployment and repatriation of contingents and assets to and from the mission area.

37.	Key initiatives, including the contractual ceiling individual rate adjustment and relocation of aviation crew, will sustain support for critical functions, consolidate services and enhance operational resilience. Utility and resource optimization efforts will promote sustainability, reduce operating costs and align with United Nations environmental objectives. Reductions in civilian staffing may affect surge capacity and workloads. To mitigate associated risks, the mission has reinforced internal coordination, oversight and prioritization mechanisms and will continue to monitor impacts and take corrective action, as necessary. Resources will remain focused on critical mandate-related tasks, with strengthened coordination between civilian and uniformed components to ensure continuity of operations.
D. 	Regional mission cooperation

38.	In 2024/25, UNSOS continued to strengthen collaboration with the African Union Commission, United Nations entities and international partners. The strategic coordination of aviation assets with United Nations Headquarters and the United Nations Logistics Base at Brindisi, Italy, enabled optimized air fleet use and ensured the timely deployment and redeployment of uniformed personnel, even in high-risk areas, in support of AUSSOM operations.

39.	UNSOS continued to utilize the Regional Service Centre in Entebbe, Uganda, for payroll, vendor payments, human resources and information technology support, which were centralized, improving turnaround times and accountability. UNSOS continued to use the Global Procurement Support Section in Entebbe to streamline procurement services, reducing delivery timelines and increasing responsiveness.

40.	For the 2026/27 period, UNSOS will continue the same regional cooperation in the 2024/25 period. UNSOS will continue to leverage regional and global United Nations support to enhance operational effectiveness, including information technology and asset management from the United Nations Logistics Base, streamlined procurement through the Global Procurement Support Section in Entebbe and strategic aviation coordination with United Nations Headquarters and the Logistics Base for aircraft contractual arrangements and troop rotation.

41.	The mission will also maintain proactive regional engagement with partners such as the African Union Commission and the Intergovernmental Authority on Development to strengthen collaboration and support mission objectives.
E.	Human resources
Component: provision of logistical support
a	Funded under general temporary assistance.
F.	Proposed staffing changes

42.	It is proposed that the position of Chief of Mine Action Programme (D-1) in Mogadishu, funded under general temporary assistance, be retained to ensure continued strategic and operational leadership of mine action activities. The position is required to provide authoritative strategic, technical and policy advice to UNSOS senior leadership, oversee the planning and implementation of the mine action workplan and coordinate engagement with national authorities, the United Nations country team and other partners. Given the continued threat posed by explosive hazards and the increasing emphasis on national ownership and coordination following the transition from UNSOM/UNTMIS, retention of the position is necessary.

43.	The Senior Programme Management Officer (P-5), funded under general temporary assistance, was established on 1 July 2023 to lead policy coordination on the security transition in collaboration with the United Nations country team and relevant UNSOS stakeholders. In view of the continuation of the programme during the 2026/27 period, it is proposed that the position be retained in order to provide strategic planning, coordination and stakeholder engagement, including direct liaison with the Federal Government of Somalia, AUSSOM and international partners, and to ensure the coherence of transition-related activities across UNSOS pillars and sections.

44.	In addition, the mission proposes the establishment of nine human rights officer posts effective 1 November 2026 to ensure the continued implementation of the human rights due diligence policy following the withdrawal of UNTMIS. The proposed staffing comprises one Senior Human Rights Officer (P-5), serving as team leader and based in Mogadishu; two posts of Human Rights Officer (P-3), based in Kismayo and Jowhar; and six posts of Human Rights Officer (National Professional Officer), two each in Mogadishu, Kismayo and Jowhar. The proposed posts represent the minimum capacity required to deliver core functions relating to the policy, including human rights risk assessment and mitigation, monitoring, analysis and reporting, advisory and decision-support services, and coordination of policy mechanisms across the mission and the wider United Nations system in Somalia.

45.	The Senior Human Rights Officer (P-5) will serve as team leader and be responsible for the overall management and strategic direction of the human rights due diligence policy programme. The incumbent will act as the principal adviser on matters relating to the policy to the Assistant Secretary-General of UNSOS and the Resident Coordinator and Humanitarian Coordinator, as well as to other United Nations entities. The incumbent will provide policy advice and technical guidance on implementation of the policy, including the preparation of risk assessments and the development, implementation and monitoring of mitigation measures. The Senior Human Rights Officer will lead engagement with Somali authorities, Somali security forces, AUSSOM and international security partners and will oversee the policy’s secretariat and analysis team, as well as supervise the regional monitoring and engagement teams. Taking into account 2 posts of Programme Management Officer (P-4 and National Professional Officer) already reflected in the current UNSOS staffing table, 2 posts of Human Rights Officer (National Professional Officer) to be provided through the United Nations country team and 9 additional posts to be established within UNSOS, the function relating to the policy will comprise 13 posts as from 1 November 2026.

46.	Three regional monitoring and engagement teams will be established. Two teams will each comprise one Human Rights Officer (P-3), serving as team leader, and two posts of Human Rights Officer (National Professional Officer). The third team, covering Mogadishu/Banaadir, will comprise two posts of Human Rights Officer (National Professional Officer) under the direct supervision of the Senior Human Rights Officer (P-5). The teams will conduct field-level monitoring of United Nations-supported security forces, assess human rights due diligence policy-related risks, verify compliance with approved risk-mitigation measures and engage with relevant stakeholders. Their findings will inform centralized risk assessments relating to the policy and support senior-level decision-making.

47.	In the light of the anticipated closure of the UNTMIS by the end of its mandate on 31 October 2026, 25 posts and positions (17 international staff and 8 international United Nations Volunteers) have been proposed for abolishment as from 1 November 2026. These positions had been dedicated to providing administrative and operational support to UNTMIS on a cost-recovery basis in accordance with Security Council resolution 2767 (2024).

48.	The mission also proposes the abolishment of 14 international posts within mission support components, several of which have been vacant for a prolonged period. The functions previously performed by these posts have been redistributed and absorbed by existing staff within the current mission support structure. The mission will monitor the impact on service delivery, processing timelines and staff well-being to assess the sustainability of this arrangement and identify any further efficiency measures, where feasible. In addition, seven international United Nations Volunteers positions were abolished under the same resource’s optimization exercise.

49.	Furthermore, the mission is proposing the nationalization of 12 posts and positions, comprising the following: (a) conversion of 3 Field Service posts to 2 National Professional Officer and 1 national General Service posts; (b) conversion of 5 national General Service posts to 1 national United Nations Volunteer positions; (c) conversion of 1 National Professional Officer post to 1 national United Nations Volunteer position; and (d) conversion of 3 international United Nations Volunteer positions to national United Nations Volunteer positions.
Human resources: proposed changes

children.text: G.	Results-based budgeting frameworks
Component: Provision of logistical support to the African Union Support and Stabilization Mission in Somalia

table_content.text: 1.1	Rapid, effective, efficient and responsible delivery of the United Nations logistics support package to AUSSOM and the Somali security forces

1.1.1	Average annual percentage of female international civilian staff (2022/23: 33.0 per cent; 2023/24: 34.0 per cent; 2024/25: 34.0 per cent; 2025/26: ≥ 35.0 per cent; 2026/27: ≥ 35.0 per cent (recruitment freeze impact))

children.text: Evaluation activities
50.	During the 2024/25 period, no evaluation of UNSOS was conducted.
51.	A self-evaluation, on a topic to be determined, is planned for the 2026/27 period
A.	Overall
(Thousands of United States dollars; budget year is from 1 July to 30 June)
B.	Non-budgeted contributions
52.	The value of non-budgeted contributions for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 is as follows:
(Thousands of United States dollars)
a	Representing the rental value of land and buildings, airport fees and landing rights.
C.	Efficiency gains
53.	The cost estimates for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 take into account the following efficiency initiatives:
(Thousands of United States dollars)

table_content.text: Investment since inception to generate the efficiency gains of 2026/27
Cumulated efficiency gains since inception and before 2026/27
Cumulated investments and efficiency gain as of 2026/27
Nationalization of 3 international posts to national General Service and conversion of 6 national posts and 3 international UNV to 9 national UNV
Abolishment of 14 international posts and 7 International UNV

Investment in renewable energy through the installation of 7 solar panel sets, which will, after commissioning, reduce fuel consumption by the equivalent of 214,620 litres per year based on 1 year of energy production

Suspension of water production during off-peak hours and increased use of recycled wastewater for non-potable purposes, which helps to lower fuel consumption, reduce maintenance costs and minimize environmental impact

Discontinuation of United Nations Support Office in Somalia support to community radio stations and strategic communication services, where monitoring and evaluation was changed from a monthly to a single annual public perception survey

Efficiencies were achieved by optimizing satellite Internet services provided by the United Nations Logistics Base at Brindisi, Italy, through improved bandwidth utilization and the consolidation of and reduction in mobile and satellite subscriptions, while maintaining required connectivity

Cost efficiencies by lowering telephony costs, bringing radio maintenance in-house and leveraging technology and renewable energy, thereby reducing fuel, maintenance and outsourcing expenses without affecting mandate delivery
Termination of the contract for 2 fixed-wing and 2 rotary-wing aircraft and related aviation fuel
Cost efficiencies were achieved by consolidating aviation crew accommodation and catering services into United Nations Support Office in Somalia facilities
Other supplies, services and equipment: mine action

Mine action support was streamlined through a reduction from 35 to 27 posts, achieved by consolidating functions and reclassifying posts to align with the mine action office’s delivery model and transition priorities
Other supplies, services and equipment: mine action

Streamlining of the explosive detection dog contracts from 2 to 1 and a reduction in the scope and scale of contracted services for counter-improvised explosive device training and mentoring provided to the African Union Support and Stabilization Mission in Somalia and Somali security forces
Assets received from the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali
Efficiencies as a percentage of the approved budget for 2025/26
Efficiencies as a percentage of the approved budget for 2025/26

children.text: D.	Unencumbered balance

Trend of the unencumbered balance

E.	Cancellation of prior-period obligations
55.	Prior-period obligations for the periods from 2022/23 to 2024/25 were cancelled as follows:
Trend of the cancellation of prior-period obligations
(Thousands of United States dollars)

Note: The amounts represent the total obligations that were cancelled at the end of the 12-month period following the end of the budget period in respect of goods supplied and services rendered in the budget period, and at the end of an additional period of four years in respect of obligations to troop- and police-contributing countries.
F.	Other revenue and adjustments, and borrowing
1.	Other revenue and adjustments
(Thousands of United States dollars)
a	Breakdown of other/miscellaneous revenue: refund of prior-period expenses ($126,600); and other revenue (-$88,900).

56.	Credits comprising the unencumbered balances, other revenue and the cancellation of prior-period obligations reduce the assessed contributions owed by Member States in the following financial year. The credit for the 2024/25 period for overall peacekeeping operations amounts to $335.5 million, including the support entities and the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali. In the current financial situation, offsetting this large amount of credit against a future assessment for the 2026/27 period will reduce the collection and undermine the ability of peacekeeping operations to fully implement their mandates. Accordingly, the Secretary-General proposes that the General Assembly approve that the credits in respect of the financial period 2024/25 be placed in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the implementation of those mandates and allow Member States to apply their share towards current or future dues once the Assembly determines that the liquidity situation has sufficiently improved. This proposal is explained in more detail in the peacekeeping overview report.
2.	Borrowing
57.	Owing to its cash liquidity situation, the mission received loans from the Peacekeeping Reserve Fund and other active peacekeeping missions, with the month-end loan balance as follows:
(Millions of United States dollars)

Abbreviations: MONUSCO, United Nations Organization Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of Congo; UNDOF, United Nations Disengagement Observer Force; UNFICYP, United Nations Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus; UNIFIL, United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon.
G.	Vacancy factors

58.	The cost estimates for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 take into account the vacancy factors outlined in the table below. The table also summarizes, by category, the actual rates in the periods 2022/23 to 2024/25, as well as the approved and current rates for the 2025/26 period.
(Percentage)
a	Funded under general temporary assistance.

59.	The vacancy factors take into account the high number of abolishments included in the proposed budget for 2026/27. Accordingly, the proposed vacancy rates for civilian personnel categories with abolishments were estimated using the total number of vacant posts and positions as at 31 December 2025, net of the number of posts and positions proposed for abolishment. For categories without proposed abolishments, the standard policy guidance was applied, using either the 12-month average vacancy rate from January to December 2025 or the actual vacancy rate as at 31 December 2025. For military and police personnel, the vacancy factors reflect their planned deployment during the period. This approach ensures that vacancy rates are based on, to the extent possible, the actual data while maintaining consistency across the proposed budgets. A vacancy rate of 50 per cent has been applied in the calculation of costs for the proposed establishments and conversions.
H.	Analysis of requirements for air operations

60.	The flight hour utilization rates for the periods from 2022/23 to 2024/25, the approved and proposed number of flight hours for the 2025/26 and 2026/27 periods, respectively, and the number of flight hours calculated on the basis of the new policy guidance are as follows:

61.	The mission proposes a total of 3 fixed-wing aircraft, achieved through the termination of 2 aircraft following the closure of UNTMIS, and 23 rotary-wing aircraft, comprising 17 military and 6 commercial helicopters, with 2 civilian helicopters having been terminated. Of the 17 military rotary-wing assets, 11 are budgeted using a 50 per cent delayed deployment factor, in line with AUSSOM operational reconfiguration planning assumptions. The proposed civilian aviation configuration reflects the mission’s operational environment, where prolonged rainy seasons and limited road infrastructure necessitate the use of air assets to gain access to some locations. The total proposed flight hours are below the levels indicated in policy guidance and are based on projected operational requirements for the 2026/27 period. The lower utilization rate of helicopters is attributable primarily to deployment delays and technical constraints affecting military rotary-wing aircraft.
62.	The estimated resource requirements for programmatic activities for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027, compared with previous periods, are as follows:
(Thousands of United States dollars)
63.	Detailed information on programmatic activities undertaken during the 2024/25 period, as well as on activities planned for the 2026/27 period, is provided in the supplementary information.

64.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) rations, due to the continued deployment of 11,622 ATMIS/AUSSOM personnel throughout the 2024/25 budget period, as authorized by the Security Council in its resolutions 2748 (2024), compared with the budgeted 9,586 ATMIS/AUSSOM personnel; (b) an unanticipated claim for hostile action; and (c) higher-than-budgeted contingent-owned equipment transportation costs, resulting from the ATMIS/AUSSOM drawdown. The increased requirements were offset in part by lower-level self-sustainment standards compared with the budgeted standards.

65.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) additional costs related to the freight costs associated with the anticipated repatriation of military equipment and its subsequent replacement from a different troop-contributing country and the deployment and repatriation of military personnel, in accordance with the planned AUSSOM reconfiguration; and (b) the higher average price per round-trip ticket, based on the actual letter of authorization expenditure pattern and the current market situation, compared with the average price used in the approved budget. This is offset in part by reduced contingent-owned equipment costs resulting from a change in force composition and the shift in unit structure, including the removal of equipment associated with infantry battalions.

66.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were due primarily to the higher actual average vacancy rate of 19.2 per cent compared with the budgeted rate of 2.9 per cent, owing to the delayed deployment of the newly reconfigured AUSSOM police.

67.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) the postponement of planned formed police unit rotations due to the planned AUSSOM reconfiguration; and (b) the non-deployment and non-functionality of major equipment during the reporting period. The reduced requirements were offset in part by an increase in cargo movements of contingent-owned equipment that were not accounted for in the approved budget.

68.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to the freight costs associated with the anticipated repatriation of equipment and its subsequent replacement from a different troop-contributing country, in accordance with the planned AUSSOM reconfiguration. The increased requirements are offset in part by reduced contingent-owned equipment costs resulting from a shift in unit structure.

69.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) a lower average vacancy rate of 7.6 per cent compared with the approved rate of 8.2 per cent; and (b) the higher salary rates based on the revised salary scale effective January 2025 compared with the rates applied in the approved budget.

70.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the application of a lower vacancy rate of 2.7 per cent compared with the rate of 7.6 per cent applied in the approved budget; (b) the establishment of three international posts (1 P-5 and 2 P-3) in order to ensure the continued and effective implementation of the human rights due diligence policy; and (c) the application of higher revised salary rates compared with the rates applied in the approved budget for the 2025/26 period.

71.	The increased requirements are offset in part by: (a) the proposed abolishment of 31 posts in line with the UNTMIS closure (17 posts) and cost-reduction measures (14 posts); and (b) the nationalization of 3 Field Service posts.

72.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) a lower vacancy rate of 14 per cent compared with the approved rate of 23.8 per cent; and (b) application of the revised salary scale effective 1 November 2024 for Kenya. The increase was offset in part by the strengthening of the Kenya shilling against the United States dollar, resulting in an actual average exchange rate of 128.86 shillings to 1 United States dollar, compared with the rate of 152.98 shillings in the approved budget.

73.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the application of the higher revised salary rates compared with the rates applied in the approved budget; (b) the conversion of two Field Service posts into two National Professional Officer posts, offset in part by the conversion of one National Professional Officer post to a national United Nations Volunteer position; and (c) the establishment of six National Professional Officer posts in order to ensure the continued and effective implementation of the human rights due diligence policy.

74.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to the application of the revised salary scale for Kenya effective 1 November 2024. The increased requirements were offset in part by: (a) the higher average vacancy rate of 14.0 per cent compared with the budgeted vacancy rate of 7.7 per cent; and (b) the strengthening of the Kenyan shilling against the United States dollar, resulting in an actual average exchange rate of 128.86 shillings to 1 United States dollar, compared with the rate of 152.98 shillings in the approved budget.

75.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the application of the higher revised salary rates compared with the rates applied in the approved budget; and (b) conversion of one Field Service post to a national General Service post. The increased requirements are offset in part by the proposed conversion of five national General Service staff to national United Nations Volunteers and the application of a higher vacancy rate of 16.7 per cent compared with the rate of 9.8 per cent applied in the approved budget.
76.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to a lower average vacancy rate of 4.8 per cent compared with the budgeted vacancy rate of 19 per cent.

77.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the proposed abolishment of 15 positions in line with the UNTMIS closure (8 positions) and the abolishment of 7 positions; and (b) a proposal for the nationalization of 3 international United Nations Volunteers to national United Nations Volunteers.
78.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to the cost of three international temporary positions due to operational requirements.

79.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to the application of a lower actual average rate of 0 per cent due to the full incumbency for the proposed retention of the position of Chief of Mine Action Programme (D-1), compared with the rate of 50.0 per cent applied in the approved budget.

80.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the engagement of fewer consultants to deliver national staff capacity-building activities, due to the prioritization of resources in response to the ongoing liquidity challenges.

81.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to a strategic shift towards the in-mission delivery of capacity-building training for national staff, which has contributed to broader staff coverage and a reduced reliance on external consultants.

82.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the lower actual number of trips compared with the budgeted estimates, owing to austerity measures to manage liquidity challenges.

83.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the lower number of trips related to official travel and training, given that staff members are expected to participate in conferences, meetings and training remotely, where possible, compared with the travel provision included in the approved budget.

84.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the strategic reprioritization of funds and austerity measures to manage the liquidity challenges, the non-acquisition of prefabricated facilities, accommodation and refrigeration equipment, engineering supplies, safety and security equipment, and lower maintenance cost compared with the budgeted estimates. The reduced requirements were offset in part by net increases in requirements to address critical security needs and ensure compliance with safety standards, including security enhancement measures, such as the upgrade of the C-RAM sense and warn system to a nine-channel system, the installation of fusion systems, closed-circuit television cameras and improvements in perimeter security infrastructure.

85.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) lower estimated construction costs for security upgrades to withstand the impact of attacks with 107 mm rockets, following the expected finalization of upgrades; (b) the lower acquisition of facilities and accommodation and safety and security-related equipment due to the availability of existing stock; and (c) lower estimated fuel consumption of 15.4 million litres of diesel fuel compared with 17.9 million litres included in the approved budget.

86.	The reduced requirements are offset in part by: (a) the inclusion of armed static security costs for UNSOS compounds, previously funded under UNTMIS, which UNSOS is expected to absorb following the end of the UNTMIS mandate on 31 October 2026; and (b) the replacement of water treatment plants that have exceeded their operational lifespan, as well as the acquisition of spare parts for water and wastewater treatment plants.

87.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) the lower actual volume of 2.9 million litres acquired at the lower actual average price of $0.950 compared with the budgeted volume of 3.2 million litres at an average price of $1.153 per litre, due to reduced vehicle rentals and the strategic reprioritization of funds to address liquidity challenges. This was offset in part by increased requirements arising from an urgent and unanticipated need for a fire truck to strengthen the mission’s fire safety and emergency response capacity.

88.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the lower estimated fuel consumption of 2.1 million litres of fuel compared with 3.2 million litres included in the approved budget; and (b) the lower projected repairs and maintenance costs following the planned write-off of 222 assets due to prolonged operation in harsh mission environments.

89.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) the lower actual volume of 6.5 million litres acquired at a lower actual average price of $0.878 compared with the budgeted volume of 12.4 million litres at an average price of $1.068 per litre; (b) lower costs for the rental and operation of fixed-wing aircraft owing to the non-utilization of the C-130 aircraft contracted for shared use across missions; and (c) the non-deployment of four military helicopters, due the reprioritization of funds to manage liquidity challenges. This was offset in part by the deployment of one uncrewed aerial system to provide aerial intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance services in support of AUSSOM as part of critical security enhancements.

90.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the termination of the contracts for two fixed-wing and two rotary-wing aircraft; (b) lower rental and operating costs of military rotary-wing aircraft based on the AUSSOM fleet reconfiguration, with cost estimates based on current contracts and the application of a 50 per cent delayed deployment factor; and (c) the lower cost for aviation crew accommodation and catering due to contract changes that are expected to eliminate external vendors and incorporate the services into existing UNSOS facilities.

91.	The reduced requirements are offset in part by the increased cost of the deployment of one uncrewed aerial system to provide aerial intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance services to AUSSOM as part of critical security enhancements due to the change in the type of uncrewed aerial system, with the application of a 50 per cent delayed deployment factor.

92.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the acquisition of fewer shipping containers compared with the budgeted estimates, due to the lower acquisition of assets and materials in response to liquidity constraints.

93.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the acquisition of a lower number of sea containers for the transportation of equipment compared with the number included in the approved budget, resulting from a lower planned acquisition.

94.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) the maintenance of ICT equipment and support services due to the cancellation of satellite imagery services and lower Internet service costs following the transition to the services provided through the United Nations Logistics Base, and the postponement of the engagement of ICT contractual personnel; (b) the non-acquisition of spare parts, due primarily to the availability of stock and liquidity challenges. The reduced requirements were offset in part by the net impact of the higher costs for internal communication services covering one satellite transponder and self-managed cloud services provided by the Logistics Base.

95.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) a reduction in public information and publication services due to the reduced scope of strategic communication services, in which monitoring and evaluation was changed from a monthly to a single annual public perception survey; (b) lower rates for centrally managed ICT services; (c) reduced satellite internet capacity through a reduction in the amount of bandwidth provided by United Nations Logistics Base; (d) the lower demand for software and licences; and (e) the lower acquisition of ICT equipment due to the availability of existing stock.

96.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the acquisition of fewer medical supplies compared with the budgeted estimates due to the availability of inventory and the strategic reprioritization of funds to manage liquidity challenges.

97.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) additional costs arising from UNSOS assuming full financial responsibility for level I+ and level III medical services following the end of the UNTMIS mandate; and (b) the acquisition of new medical equipment to replace outdated or non-functional equipment.

98.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the reprioritization of resources to respond to liquidity challenges, including: (a) the reduced scope of mine detection and mine-clearing services; (b) reduced training for national staff capacity-building; and (c) reduced planned training for the transition of ATMIS to AUSSOM in areas such as movement control, aviation, engineering, medical and predeployment.

99.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the lower costs for mine detection and mine-clearing services attributable to the reductions in third-party agreements, due to (i) the streamlining of the explosive detection dog contracts from two to one; and (ii) a reduction in the scope and scale of contracted services for counter-improvised explosive device training and mentoring provided to AUSSOM and Somali security forces; and (b) lower freight costs as a result of a reduced planned acquisition.

text: IV.	Actions to be taken by the General Assembly

children.text: 100.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly in connection with the financing of the United Nations Support Office for Somalia are:
(a)	To take note of the unencumbered balance of $13,471,300 with respect to the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025;

(b)	To take note of other revenue for the period ended 30 June 2025 amounting to $3,884,000 from investment revenue ($199,700), other/miscellaneous revenue ($37,700) and the cancellation of prior-period obligations ($3,646,600);

(c)	To approve the apportionment of the amount of $17,355,300 among Member States in accordance with the applicable scale of assessment for 2025 and to place such apportioned amount in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the full, efficient and effective implementation of mandates;

(d)	To allow Member States to apply their share of the amount apportioned in subparagraph (c) above towards current or future dues once the General Assembly determines that the liquidity situation has improved sufficiently to allow such application;
(e)	To appropriate the amount of $481,671,800 for the maintenance of the mission for the 12-month period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027;
(f)	To assess the amount in subparagraph (e) above at a monthly rate of $40,139,316 should the Security Council decide to continue the mandate of the mission.

text: V.	Summary of follow-up action taken to implement the decisions and requests of the General Assembly in its resolutions 76/274 and 79/312, including the requests and recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions endorsed by the Assembly

children.text: General Assembly – Cross-cutting issues
(Resolution 76/274)

table_content.text: Requests the Secretary-General to improve comprehensive oversight of the activities of peacekeeping missions and implement the recommendations of relevant oversight bodies in this regard to avoid deficiencies in management and related economic losses, with the aim of ensuring full compliance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations (para. 17).

As at 31 December 2025, UNSOS had implemented 19 of the 22 open recommendations of the Board of Auditors, and 3 remained under implementation. During the 2024/2025 period, there were 39 Office of Internal Oversight Services recommendations, 9 of which were closed, while 30 remained under review for implementation/closure by December 2025. Regular follow-up is undertaken to ensure the timely closure of the remaining recommendations.

Requests that future budget proposals demonstrate the scalability of mission support components, including their staffing and operational costs, be proportionate in relation to the changing level of other mission components and include standard indicators (para. 27).
The mission support ratio (total number of mission support and security personnel per 1,000 total mission personnel) in the 2025/26 period is 46.7, higher than the 27.2 in the 2020/21 period.

Requests the Secretary-General to consider options for greater nationalization of functions when formulating budget submissions, commensurate with mission mandates and requirements where applicable (para. 34).

UNSOS continues to advance its priority of nationalization through structured capacity-building initiatives. Efforts to strengthen national staff and national partner institutions, including through training and other capacity-building measures, are being implemented. Accordingly, the proposed budget for 2026/27 reflects the nationalization of selected international posts, consistent with UNSOS strategic priorities on capacity-building.

children.text: Financing of the United Nations Support Office in Somalia
(A/79/724/Add.10 and General Assembly resolution 79/312)

table_content.text: The Advisory Committee trusts that the Secretary-General will provide updated information to the General Assembly, at the time of the consideration of the present report, regarding the plan and timing of a civilian staffing review for UNSOS, in light of the independent strategic review. The Committee recalls General Assembly resolution 76/274 and considers that, as a matter of principle, the Secretary-General should ensure the continuous adaptation and optimization of the structures, including through civilian and security staffing reviews, with the aim to identify efficiencies that will be reflected in the next budget proposal (see also para. 27 below) (para. 23).

The planned civilian staffing review, scheduled for 2026, will review all posts in the mission and make recommendations on those posts that are essential for effective mandate implementation. In this context, the mission has initiated preparatory activities in close collaboration with the Human Resources Services Division. The outcomes of this preparatory process are intended to inform future UNSOS budget submissions, subject to the timing of the finalization of the review.

The Advisory Committee trusts that updated information on the status of vacant posts, as well as on the impact of the liquidity situation on projected hirings, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next budget submission (para. 24).

The number of vacant posts and positions is taken into account when determining the proposed vacancy rates. Information on long-vacant posts and positions is provided in the supplementary information, while the updated status of all vacant posts and positions is provided during the legislative review of the performance and budget report.
Vacant posts have been managed strategically and deliberately, with priority given to functions linked directly to mandate-critical support and security services.

The Advisory Committee trusts that additional information on the timeline of the new solicitation model, as well as the expected impact on fuel costs in UNSOS, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of consideration of the present report and in the next budget report (para. 34).

The solicitation process, coordinated with the Logistics Division and the Procurement Division at United Nations Headquarters, is under way and expected to be completed by March 2026. The corresponding impact on fuel costs will be assessed upon completion of the solicitation process.

The Advisory Committee notes UNSOS efforts to improve its environmental footprint and trusts that further efficiency gains will be generated from energy initiatives, as well as other environmental initiatives, and will be reflected in future reports (para. 44).

Notwithstanding the increased indirect fire affecting the mission site in Mogadishu, which necessitated the dismantling of installed solar panels, UNSOS successfully reinstalled the panels following enhancements to physical security. In addition, the mission installed an additional 244 kWp of solar capacity in Kismayo, further expanding its renewable energy base. Efforts are also under way to reconnect the Baidoa site to a private energy provider and to continue the installation of solar panels deployed in Jowhar, with a view to increasing the mission’s share of renewable energy.

UNSOS has achieved a high level of implementation of energy-efficiency measures, including the use of LED lighting, energy-efficient air-conditioning units and approximately 76 per cent synchronization of diesel generators. Complementary environmental initiatives include water reclamation across all sites to reduce freshwater consumption; the reduction of single-use plastic water bottles through the deployment of water dispensers and the installation of water bottling plants using reusable bottles; and the use of biodegradable bags and containers at post exchange facilities and cafeterias.
